sections.summary: Clarithromycin strongly inhibits carbamazepine metabolism and can precipitate carbamazepine toxicity within just a few days of co-administration.
sections.mechanism: Carbamazepine is metabolized primarily by CYP3A4, and clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor, sharply reducing carbamazepine clearance and allowing levels to accumulate rapidly.
sections.clinical_effects: Carbamazepine levels can rise significantly, producing ataxia, diplopia, dizziness, sedation, and in severe cases nystagmus or cardiac conduction abnormalities consistent with toxicity.
sections.management: Avoid clarithromycin in patients on carbamazepine when possible; azithromycin, which has minimal CYP3A4 inhibition, is a reasonable alternative macrolide. If clarithromycin is unavoidable, reduce the carbamazepine dose and monitor levels closely.
sections.monitoring: Check carbamazepine serum level before and within a few days of starting clarithromycin, and watch for early signs of toxicity such as ataxia, blurred or double vision, and drowsiness.

evidence.citation: Biaxin (clarithromycin) FDA prescribing information, Drug Interactions section
